title: The Path of Meditation
introduction: Chapter 6 of the Bhagavad Gita, Dhyana Yoga (The Yoga of Meditation), is one of the most practical and beloved chapters for modern seekers. Krishna shifts from philosophy to technique: how to actually still the restless mind, sit in meditation, and live as a yogi in the middle of everyday chaos. This is the chapter that answers the GenZ and Millennial cry: 'I know I should meditate, but my brain won't shut up!' Krishna gives clear instructions on posture, diet, routine, and — most importantly — how to befriend the mind instead of fighting it. He also addresses what happens if you fall off the path and reassures Arjuna (and us) that sincere effort is never wasted. In today's world of endless notifications, doom-scrolling, burnout, and 24/7 hustle, this chapter is pure gold: it shows how to turn meditation into a lifestyle, not just a 10-minute app session. The essence remains intact — meditation is not escape; it is the highest form of action that leads to unbreakable inner peace and self-mastery.

shlokas.sanskrit:
श्रीभगवानुवाच |
अनाश्रितः कर्मफलं कार्यं कर्म करोति यः |
स सन्न्यासी च योगी च न निरग्निर्न चाक्रियः ||
shlokas.transliteration:
śrī-bhagavān uvāca
anāśritaḥ karma-phalaṁ kāryaṁ karma karoti yaḥ
sa sannyāsī ca yogī ca na niragnir na cākriyaḥ
shlokas.translation: The Supreme Personality of Godhead said: One who is unattached to the fruits of his work and who works as he is obligated is in the renounced order of life and he is the true mystic not he who lights no fire and performs no duty.
shlokas.millennial: Krishna teaches that a person who performs his prescribed duties without attachment to the results is a true renouncer and a real yogi. Such a person does not need to give up all external activities. This verse opens the beautiful chapter on the path of meditation by showing that genuine meditation begins with selfless action. For people in their thirties who carry professional and family responsibilities this verse brings great encouragement. For example a manager who completes every assignment with full effort but without craving promotion or fearing failure is already practicing the yoga of meditation while staying active in the world. Detailed meaning: Krishna clarifies that renunciation is an inner attitude not an external show. One who works selflessly is both a sannyasi and a yogi even while performing duties. This teaching integrates action with meditation and makes the path practical for busy lives. The verse makes readers eager to see how daily work can become a meditation practice. Practice: perform your responsibilities with full presence yet without personal attachment to outcomes and feel how your mind becomes steady and meditative.
shlokas.genz: Krishna teaches that a person who does his given tasks without chasing the results is a true renouncer and a real yogi. Such a person does not need to drop everything. This verse opens the beautiful chapter on the path of meditation by showing that genuine meditation starts with selfless action. For young adults who balance studies work and social life this verse feels empowering. For example a creator who posts content with full energy but without needing likes or validation is already walking the yoga of meditation while staying engaged online. Detailed meaning: Krishna explains that renunciation is an inner state not an outer show. One who acts without clinging is both a sannyasi and a yogi even while moving through daily life. This teaching turns ordinary actions into meditation and makes the path real for modern routines. The verse makes you eager to see how your daily grind can become meditative. Practice: do your tasks with full focus yet without holding onto results and notice how your mind settles into a meditative flow.
shlokas.genalpha: Krishna teaches that a person who plays his given games without wanting the prizes is a true renouncer and a real yogi. Such a person does not need to stop all play. This verse opens the beautiful chapter on the path of meditation by showing that genuine meditation starts with selfless play. For children this is like sharing toys with a happy heart without needing to keep them. For example a child who plays with friends fully but without grabbing the win is already practicing the yoga of meditation. Detailed meaning: Krishna explains that renunciation is a happy feeling inside not an outer show. One who plays without clinging is both a renouncer and a yogi even while enjoying games. This teaching turns everyday play into meditation and makes the path fun for little ones. The verse makes you curious to see how your games can become meditative. Practice: play your games with full joy yet without wanting to keep the prizes and feel how your heart becomes calm and happy inside.

shlokas.sanskrit:
यं सन्न्यासमिति प्राहुर्योगं तं विद्धि पाण्डव |
न ह्यसन्न्यस्तसङ्कल्पो योगी भवति कश्चन ||
shlokas.transliteration:
yaṁ sannyāsam iti prāhur yogaṁ taṁ viddhi pāṇḍava
na hy asannyasta-saṅkalpo yogī bhavati kaścana
shlokas.translation: What is called renunciation by the wise is yoga O son of Pāṇḍu for no one becomes a yogi without giving up the desire for the fruits of his work.
shlokas.millennial: Krishna teaches that what the wise call renunciation is actually yoga. No one can become a yogi without first giving up the desire for results. This verse links renunciation and meditation directly. For people in their thirties who pursue long term goals this verse brings clarity. For example a professional who stops obsessing over yearly bonuses and focuses on doing the work well discovers true meditative peace in his career. Detailed meaning: Krishna shows that renunciation is not giving up work but giving up the mental clinging to outcomes. This inner shift turns ordinary duty into the path of meditation. The verse makes readers eager to release mental attachments. Practice: identify any desire for specific results in your tasks and gently release it so your daily work becomes a steady meditation.
shlokas.genz: Krishna teaches that what the wise call renunciation is actually yoga. No one can become a yogi without first dropping the chase for results. Detailed meaning: Krishna shows that renunciation is not quitting work but quitting the mental hold on outcomes. This inner shift turns daily moves into the path of meditation. The verse makes you eager to drop the chase. Practice: spot any want for specific results in your tasks and gently let it go so your daily energy becomes a steady meditation.

This inner shift turns everyday games into the path of meditation. The verse makes you curious to let go. Practice: notice any want for prizes in your games and gently release it so your play becomes a happy meditation.

shlokas.sanskrit:
आरुरुक्षोर्मुनेर्योगं कर्म कारणमुच्यते |
योगारूढस्य तस्यैव शमः कारणमुच्यते ||
shlokas.transliteration:
ārurukṣor muner yogaṁ karma kāraṇam ucyate
yogārūḍhasya tasyaiva śamaḥ kāraṇam ucyate
shlokas.translation: For one who is a neophyte in the eightfold yoga system work is said to be the means and for one who has attained to yoga cessation of all material activities is said to be the means.
shlokas.millennial: Krishna explains that for a beginner on the path of meditation work is the means while for one who has reached the stage of yoga the cessation of material activities becomes the means. This verse describes the progression. For people in their thirties starting a meditation practice this verse gives guidance. For example a busy executive who begins with mindful work gradually moves toward periods of inner silence and finds both stages helpful. Detailed meaning: Krishna outlines the two stages of the meditation path showing that action prepares the ground and stillness follows naturally. This beautiful progression makes the path of meditation accessible. The verse makes readers eager to follow the steps. Practice: start with selfless work as your meditation and allow stillness to grow naturally as you advance.

shlokas.sanskrit:
यदा हि नेन्द्रियार्थेषु न कर्मस्वनुषज्जते |
सर्वसङ्कल्पसंन्यासी योगारूढस्तदोच्यते ||
shlokas.transliteration:
yadā hi nendriyārtheṣu na karmasv anuṣajjate
sarva-saṅkalpa-sannyāsī yogārūḍhas tadocyate
shlokas.translation: When a person is no longer attached to the objects of the senses or to the results of his work and when he has renounced all desires he is said to have reached the stage of yoga.
shlokas.millennial: Krishna states that when one is no longer attached to sense objects or to the results of work and has renounced all desires he is said to have reached yoga. This verse defines the advanced stage. For people in their thirties who have built careers this verse shows the sign of progress. For example a leader who performs duties excellently yet feels no pull toward status or possessions has truly attained the yoga stage. Detailed meaning: Krishna describes the inner freedom that marks the completion of the meditation path. This beautiful definition helps one recognize genuine advancement. The verse makes readers eager to cultivate this detachment. Practice: observe your attachments to senses and results and gently release them so you reach the stage where yoga becomes natural.

shlokas.sanskrit:
उद्धरेदात्मनात्मानं नात्मानमवसादयेत् |
आत्मैव ह्यात्मनो बन्धुरात्मैव रिपुरात्मनः ||
shlokas.transliteration:
uddhared ātmanātmānaṁ nātmānam avasādayet
ātmaiva hy ātmano bandhur ātmaiva ripur ātmanaḥ
shlokas.translation: One must deliver himself with the help of his own mind and not degrade himself. The mind is the friend of the conditioned soul and the mind is the enemy of the conditioned soul.
shlokas.millennial: Krishna advises that one should elevate the self by the mind and never degrade it. The mind can be the best friend or the worst enemy. This verse emphasizes self responsibility in meditation. For people in their thirties building self discipline this verse is empowering. For example a person who trains the mind through daily reflection turns it into a supportive friend that helps during tough decisions. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches that the meditation path requires using the mind to lift the self rather than letting it pull one down. This beautiful reminder places the power in your hands. The verse makes readers eager to befriend the mind. Practice: train your mind with positive focus each day so it becomes your greatest ally on the meditation path.

shlokas.sanskrit:

shlokas.translation: For one who has conquered the mind and is peaceful the Supreme is already attained. Such a person is equipoised in cold and heat happiness and distress and honor and dishonor.

shlokas.sanskrit:

shlokas.transliteration:
jñāna-vijñāna-tṛptātmā kūṭa-stho vijitendriyaḥ
yukta ity ucyate yogī sama-loṣṭāśma-kāñcanaḥ
shlokas.translation: One who is satisfied with knowledge and realization who is equipoised and who has controlled the senses is said to be a yogi. Such a person is called united with the Supreme and he sees a lump of earth a stone and gold as the same.
shlokas.millennial: Krishna describes the yogi who is satisfied with knowledge and realization who is steady and who has mastered the senses. Such a person sees earth stone and gold as equal. For example a scholar who treats all material things with the same even vision remains undisturbed by wealth or loss. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches that true yoga brings satisfaction in knowledge and equal vision toward all objects. This beautiful state marks the completion of the meditation path. The verse makes readers eager to develop this equal sight. Practice: cultivate knowledge until you see all material things as equal so your mind stays steady and satisfied.

shlokas.transliteration:
suhṛn-mitrāry-udāsīna-madhyastha-dveṣya-bandhuṣu
sādhuṣv api ca pāpeṣu sama-buddhir viśiṣyate
shlokas.translation: A person is considered still further advanced when he regards honest well wishers affectionate benefactors neutral mediators envious enemies relatives and sinners with an equal mind.
shlokas.millennial: Krishna says a person is more advanced when he sees the same in well wishers friends enemies neutral parties relatives and even sinners. This verse expands equal vision. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches that advanced meditation produces equal mindedness toward all beings. This beautiful quality is the sign of a superior yogi. The verse makes readers eager to practice this equality. Practice: view every person you meet with the same even heart so your meditation deepens into universal love.

shlokas.transliteration:

shlokas.sanskrit:
शुचौ देशे प्रतिष्ठाप्य स्थिरमासनमात्मनः |
नात्युच्छ्रितं नातिनीचं चैलाजिनकुशोत्तरम् ||
shlokas.transliteration:
śucau deśe pratiṣṭhāpya sthiram āsanam ātmanaḥ

shlokas.translation: To practice yoga one should go to a clean secluded place and set up a firm seat that is neither too high nor too low and that is covered first with a grass mat then a deerskin and then a cloth.
shlokas.millennial: Krishna describes the ideal physical setup for meditation. One should choose a clean solitary spot and prepare a stable seat that is not too elevated or too low and that is layered with sacred coverings. This verse begins the practical instructions for the path of meditation. For people in their thirties who juggle demanding schedules this verse offers a realistic way to create a personal sanctuary. For example a working parent who clears a quiet corner of the home and arranges a simple cushion with a mat feels ready to sit for even ten minutes of focused meditation after a long day. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches that the external environment supports inner stillness. A proper seat helps the body remain steady so the mind can turn inward. This practical guidance shows that meditation is accessible within daily life. The verse makes readers eager to prepare their own space. Practice: choose a clean quiet spot in your home set up a stable seat and sit there regularly so your meditation practice becomes steady and deep.

saṅkalpa-prabhavān kāmāṁs tyaktvā sarvān aśeṣataḥ
manasā vividhān sarvān yuñjyāt yogam ātmanaḥ
shlokas.translation: One should abandon without exception all mental desires born of sankalpa and control the mind from all directions to fix it on the self.
shlokas.millennial: Krishna instructs to completely give up all mental desires born of imagination and control the mind from every side to fix it on the self. This verse gives the method of mind control. For people in their thirties battling wandering thoughts this verse provides clear direction. For example someone who notices daydreams of future success and gently returns the mind to the present self finds growing inner focus. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches that renouncing all mental fantasies and directing the mind inward is essential for meditation. This practical step leads to self union. The verse makes readers eager to master the mind. Practice: catch every wandering desire and bring the mind back to the self so meditation becomes steady.
shlokas.genz: Krishna instructs to drop every mental desire born of imagination and control the mind from all sides to fix it on the self. This verse gives the method of mind control. shlokas.sanskrit:
शनैः शनै रूपरमेद् बुद्ध्या धृतिगृहीतया |
आत्मसंस्थं मनः कृत्वा न किञ्चिदपि चिन्तयेत् ||
shlokas.transliteration:
śanaiḥ śanair uparamed buddhyā dhṛti-gṛhītayā
ātma-saṁsthaṁ manaḥ kṛtvā na kiñcid api cintayet
shlokas.translation: Gradually step by step one should become situated in trance by means of intelligence controlled by firm determination. One should fix the mind on the self and think of nothing else.
shlokas.millennial: Krishna advises to gradually still the mind through steady intelligence and firm determination fixing it on the self and thinking of nothing else. This verse gives the gradual method. For people in their thirties building habits this verse offers patience. For example someone who starts with five minutes of focused breathing and slowly increases time develops strong meditation. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches slow steady progress with determined intelligence leads to deep trance. This beautiful gradual approach makes meditation sustainable. The verse makes readers eager to begin step by step. Practice: increase meditation time gradually with firm resolve and fix the mind only on the self.
shlokas.genz: Krishna advises to gradually still the mind through steady intelligence and firm determination fixing it on the self and thinking of nothing else. This verse gives the gradual method. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches slow steady progress with determined intelligence leads to deep trance. This beautiful gradual approach makes meditation sustainable. shlokas.sanskrit:
यतो यतो निश्चरति मनश्चञ्चलमस्थिरम् |
ततस्ततो नियम्यैतदात्मन्येव वशं नयेत् ||
shlokas.transliteration:
yato yato niścarati manaś cañcalam asthiram
tatas tato niyamyaitad ātmany eva vaśaṁ nayet
shlokas.translation: From whatever place the mind wanders due to its flickering and unsteady nature one must certainly withdraw it and bring it back under the control of the self.
shlokas.millennial: Krishna instructs that whenever the unsteady mind wanders one must bring it back and fix it under the control of the self. This verse gives the core technique of mind control. For people in their thirties with racing thoughts this verse is practical. For example someone whose mind drifts during work gently returns it to the present task and builds concentration. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches constant redirection of the wandering mind to the self as the way to mastery. This beautiful practice is essential for meditation. The verse makes readers eager to train the mind. Practice: whenever your mind wanders notice it and gently bring it back to the self so concentration grows strong.
shlokas.genz: Krishna instructs that whenever the restless mind wanders one must bring it back and fix it under the control of the self. This verse gives the core technique of mind control. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches constant redirection of the wandering mind to the self as the way to mastery. This beautiful practice is essential for meditation. The verse makes you eager to train the mind. Practice: whenever your mind wanders notice it and gently bring it back to the self so focus grows strong.

shlokas.sanskrit:
प्रशान्तमनसं ह्येनं योगिनं सुखमुत्तमम् |
उपैति शान्तरजसं ब्रह्मभूतमकल्मषम् ||
shlokas.transliteration:
praśānta-manasaṁ hy enaṁ yoginaṁ sukham uttamam
upaiti śānta-rajasam brahma-bhūtam akalmaṣam
shlokas.translation: The yogi whose mind is peaceful whose passions are quiet who is free from sin and who has become one with Brahman attains the highest happiness.
shlokas.millennial: Krishna assures that the yogi with peaceful mind free from passion sinless and united with Brahman attains supreme happiness. This verse shows the reward of meditation. For people in their thirties seeking joy this verse gives hope. For example someone who reaches calm through practice feels a deep happiness that remains steady. Detailed meaning: Krishna describes the state of perfect peace and union with Brahman as the highest joy. This beautiful result comes from successful meditation. The verse makes readers eager to reach this happiness. Practice: calm your mind through meditation until passion fades and union with the divine brings supreme happiness.
shlokas.genz: Krishna assures that the yogi with peaceful mind free from passion sinless and united with Brahman attains supreme happiness. This verse shows the reward of meditation. shlokas.sanskrit:
युञ्जन्नेवं सदात्मानं योगी विगतकल्मषः |
सुखेन ब्रह्मसंस्पर्शमत्यन्तं सुखमश्नुते ||
shlokas.transliteration:
yuñjann evaṁ sadātmānaṁ yogī vigata-kalmaṣaḥ
sukhena brahma-saṁsparśam atyantaṁ sukham aśnute
shlokas.translation: Thus the yogi constantly engaged in the practice of yoga becomes free from all impurities and easily attains infinite happiness by touching the Supreme Brahman.
shlokas.millennial: Krishna assures that the yogi who constantly practices yoga becomes free from impurities and easily touches infinite happiness in Brahman. This verse promises the result of steady practice. For people in their thirties committed to growth this verse brings motivation. For example someone who meditates daily finds impurities lift and infinite joy appears. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches that constant yoga practice purifies completely and brings effortless union with infinite bliss. This beautiful promise is the fruit of the meditation path. The verse makes readers eager to stay consistent. Practice: keep your meditation steady every day so impurities fade and infinite happiness touches you.
shlokas.genz: Krishna assures that the yogi who constantly practices yoga becomes free from impurities and easily touches infinite happiness in Brahman. shlokas.sanskrit:
सर्वं विश्वमयं चात्मानं सर्वं चात्मनि संनादति |
सर्वं चात्मनि संनादति सर्वं चात्मनि संनादति ||
shlokas.transliteration:
sarvaṁ viśvamayaṁ cātmānaṁ sarvaṁ cātmani saṁnādati
sarvaṁ cātmani saṁnādati sarvaṁ cātmani saṁnādati
shlokas.translation: The yogi who is united with the Supreme sees the self in all beings and all beings in the self everywhere he looks.
shlokas.millennial: Krishna describes the yogi who sees the self in all beings and all beings in the self everywhere. This verse reveals the vision of oneness. For people in their thirties seeking connection this verse brings unity. For example a teacher who sees the divine in every student and every student in the divine teaches with profound love. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches that advanced meditation reveals the unity of all existence. This beautiful vision is the fruit of the path. The verse makes readers eager to attain this sight. Practice: meditate until you see the self in everyone and everyone in the self so unity fills your life.
shlokas.genz: Krishna describes the yogi who sees the self in all beings and all beings in the self everywhere. This verse reveals the vision of oneness. shlokas.sanskrit:
यो मां पश्यति सर्वत्र सर्वं च मयि पश्यति |
तस्याहं न प्रणश्यामि स च मे न प्रणश्यति ||
shlokas.transliteration:
yo māṁ paśyati sarvatra sarvaṁ ca mayi paśyati
tasyāhaṁ na praṇaśyāmi sa ca me na praṇaśyati
shlokas.translation: For one who sees Me everywhere and sees everything in Me I am never lost to him nor is he ever lost to Me.
shlokas.millennial: Krishna teaches that one who sees the divine in every person and every situation and sees every person and situation in the divine never loses connection with the Supreme. This verse reveals the vision of oneness in the path of meditation. For people in their thirties who interact with diverse teams and clients this verse transforms relationships. For example a manager who sees the same divine presence in every colleague from intern to CEO builds genuine empathy and leads with wisdom that inspires loyalty. Detailed meaning: Krishna shows that the meditation path culminates in constant awareness of the Supreme in all things. This equal vision ends separation and brings unbreakable union. The verse makes readers eager to cultivate this seeing. Practice: look for the divine spark in every person you meet today so you never feel alone and your meditation flows into daily life.

shlokas.sanskrit:
सर्वभूतस्थितं यो मां भजत्येकत्वमास्थितः |

shlokas.sanskrit:
आत्मौपम्येन सर्वत्र समं पश्यति योऽर्जुन |
सुखं वा यदि वा दुःखं स योगी परमो मतः ||
shlokas.transliteration:
ātmaupamyena sarvatra samaṁ paśyati yo ’rjuna
sukhaṁ vā yadi vā duḥkhaṁ sa yogī paramo mataḥ
shlokas.translation: O Arjuna he who sees equality in happiness and distress by comparing them to his own self is the highest yogi.
shlokas.millennial: Krishna declares that one who sees the same self in happiness and distress and treats them equally is the highest yogi. This verse defines the supreme meditator. For people in their thirties facing success and setbacks this verse offers balance. For example a leader who treats praise and criticism with the same even mind leads with wisdom that endures. Detailed meaning: Krishna teaches that the highest meditation produces equal vision toward pleasure and pain by seeing the self in both. This state marks the peak of the path. The verse makes readers eager to attain this equality. Practice: view happiness and distress as the same by seeing the self in both so you become the highest yogi.

shlokas.sanskrit:
अर्जुन उवाच |
योऽयं योगस्त्वया प्रोक्तः साम्येन मधुसूदन |

shlokas.transliteration:
arjuna uvāca
yo ’yaṁ yogas tvayā proktaḥ sāmyena madhusūdana
etasyāhaṁ na paśyāmi cañcalatvāt sthitiṁ sthirām
shlokas.translation: Arjuna said: O Madhusudana this yoga of equanimity which you have described appears difficult to me because the mind is very unsteady.
shlokas.millennial: Arjuna confesses that the yoga of equanimity described by Krishna seems impossible because the mind is restless. This verse shows Arjuna's honest doubt. For people in their thirties who struggle with a wandering mind this verse is relatable. For example a student or professional who tries meditation but finds thoughts racing understands Arjuna's concern. Detailed meaning: Arjuna expresses the common difficulty of controlling the unsteady mind in the path of meditation. This honest question opens the way for Krishna to give practical solutions. The verse makes readers eager for the answer. Practice: acknowledge the restlessness of your mind with honesty so you can learn the techniques to steady it.

shlokas.sanskrit:
चञ्चलं हि मनः कृष्ण प्रमाथि बलवद् दृढम् |
तस्याहं निग्रहं मन्ये वायोरिव सुदुष्करम् ||
shlokas.transliteration:

The verse makes readers eager for the method. Practice: accept the strong restless nature of the mind so you can apply the techniques Krishna will teach.

shlokas.sanskrit:
श्रीभगवानुवाच |
असंशयं महाबाहो मनो दुर्निग्रहं चलम् |
अभ्यासेन तु कौन्तेय वैराग्येण च गृह्यते ||
shlokas.transliteration:
śrī-bhagavān uvāca

abhyāsena tu kaunteya vairāgyeṇa ca gṛhyate
shlokas.translation: The Supreme Personality of Godhead said: O mighty armed one it is undoubtedly very difficult to curb the restless mind but it is possible by suitable practice and by detachment.
shlokas.millennial: Krishna acknowledges that the mind is indeed hard to control but it can be mastered through regular practice and detachment. This verse gives hope and the method. For people in their thirties building discipline this verse is encouraging. For example someone who starts small daily meditation and gradually detaches from distractions succeeds in steadying the mind. Detailed meaning: Krishna confirms the challenge yet provides the two keys of abhyasa and vairagya. This beautiful assurance motivates consistent effort. The verse makes readers eager to begin the practice. Practice: start regular meditation and cultivate detachment from distractions so the restless mind becomes calm.
shlokas.genz: Krishna acknowledges that the mind is indeed hard to control but it can be mastered through regular practice and detachment. This verse gives hope and the method. shlokas.sanskrit:
असंयतात्मना योगो दुष्प्राप इति मे मतिः |

shlokas.transliteration:

vaśyātmanā tu yatatā śakyo ’vāptum upāyataḥ
shlokas.translation: For one whose mind is unbridled yoga is difficult to attain but for one who has controlled the mind and who strives by right means it is possible.
shlokas.millennial: Krishna states that yoga is hard for one whose mind is uncontrolled but possible for one who has mastered the mind and strives properly. This verse motivates self mastery. For people in their thirties developing habits this verse gives realistic hope. For example someone who gradually controls wandering thoughts through daily practice finds meditation attainable. shlokas.translation: The Supreme Personality of Godhead said: O son of Pṛthā there is no destruction for him either in this world or the next. No one who performs auspicious activities meets with misfortune O friend.
